speaker: interviewer
text: --six and we're interviewing Jane Lester. I'd like to know what you've been doing in the years since.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in the army, a US Army employee, and always employed in Europe, primarily in the Frankfurt area, until I retired. And-- but we went home frequently on TDY, or for home leave. And so we didn't-- weren't expected to lose touch with America. You know, we were an American enclave, you might say, in a camp behind barbed wire, doing our work in Germany, going on T-- temporary duty here or there, traveling. And after I retired-- I had always, in my private life, I'd always helped Dr. Kempner as much as I could. Not so much with his law practice, as with what he did as his passion in life, namely participating, encouraging, working with the war crimes trials run by the German government, of which there were very many. Nuremberg didn't encompass the war criminals. And these trials took place, 10 or 12 of them. They are all reported, very interestingly, in his memoirs. And they meant traveling to wherever the trial was. And they meant interrogate-- he took on a role. He was not a German. But he took on a role-- he was a naturalized American and a member of the German Bar, called [GERMAN], which doesn't really exist in-- in American law. He was not employed by the German government. But he was allowed to participate as the lawyer of somebody who had been damaged by that war criminal. So it would be some Israeli family. Or in the case of Edith Stein and Anne Frank, relatives of those two had been damaged by the three war criminals that were trialed-- tried in Munich for their war crimes in Holland, in killing-- he wrote a book about that, Anne Frank-- 'Edith Stein und Anne Frank, Two of a Hundred Thousand.' Because he thought-- in German, alas. In German, in Ivrit, in Dutch. But not in English, unfortunately. There's so much of his work that should be translated into English. It is so authentic. He had-- his life was research on anti-Nazism and the deeds of the Nazis. And he has written many wonderful book. 'Eichmann und Komplizen,' 'Eichmann and His Accomplices,' published in 1961, in German, was, more or less, the indictment and the trial program in the Eichmann process in Jerusalem, where he went as an expert witness.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where he helped them in so many ways, you know? Because they didn't have all that background. They didn't have all those documents. And he could tell them where they were. Did you participate in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I helped him. I helped him. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a lot to that, writing letters. He had a big staff to write letters in his law office. But not people who knew English, write to English archives and English language archives in Washington, and things like that. But there were-- the man was always busy. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had fun too. He had fun too. He enjoyed the-- the new world in Germany. He enjoyed watching the development of the German government in Bonn. He had close friends in the government in Bonn, very close friends. He would go to Adenauer. I remember one thing he tells in his memoirs. Adenauer, he said, well, you really took the burden off our shoulders in Nuremberg. He meant the Nuremberg Trials had accomplished something that for the baby German government, the newly-organized West German government, would have been very difficult to accomplish, you know, with all the pressures still rolling inside Germany, you know, the new Germany. And he went off into Bonn, had friends there. And traveled throughout America. Traveled in Brooklyn-- went to Brooklyn, made lectures all over to inform them on the Nuremberg Trials, and to inform them on the prospects for a good German constitution and German law that was developing. The Restitution Law he worked very hard on, talking things over with the German organizing-- newly-organized German government and everything. He spent about half of his time in Germany, and half of his time at his very nice home in suburban Philadelphia, where he also had an office. One of the ladies who was his assistant, first in Florence, and then as a refugee in Philadelphia, and worked with him right up to the day-- she was with him when he died, as I was. And-- he worked all the time, and he enjoyed his work tremendously. And he enjoyed his spare time very much, very much, going to Switzerland or different things that he did. Well, his wife gave him a great deal of happiness. She did something that he had in mind in Nuremberg. He saw that Hitler had wanted-- had a big program to punish all the Catholic hierarchy and people. And Hitler, in some table talk he had, said we have to-- as soon as we get-- more or less, as soon as we get through with the Jew-- oh, wait a minute. Somebody said, we should-- we Germans-- to Hitler-- to Hitler. We Germans should make a model of the ancient people, the classic Rome and Greece.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he said. Not at all. That's not right, he said. They gave us the true most vicious things, you know, in history. Catholicism and the-- and what is it? Catholicism and syphilis. That's quoted from Hitler in one of these-- friends of Hitler reported after the war, you know? And Dr. Kempner said that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he found so much evidence of this, the torture of the priests in Dachau, and the execution of so many priests who had opened their mouths and said he's doing things that are wrong, or had said a word for the Jews, or something. And his wife did the research, very difficult, hardworking research. 14 years, she worked. She worked in Nuremberg too, from 1947 on, a German girl from Berlin that went with him in refuge to Philadelphia. And she wrote a book called [GERMAN], only in German, alas. She did research on these priests that were abused in-- in-- in the courts of Hitler. You know, went to Dachau or-- their fates, terrible fates. Many of them killed. And they stand for something, the research shows, for more than 10,000 clergyman, most-- mostly Catholic, many Protestant clergyman. Many women, nuns, a great many nuns, who fought against Hitler [? in their way. ?] If they had orphanages in their charge, they tried to protect the so-called crippled children or mentally retarded or something from being sent off to the gas chambers, you know, and risked their lives for these victims of Hitler, you know, and paid for it with their lives, in many-- many cases. And her book has been out of print for 20 years. And we have the great good fortune that we are going to have this book. The publisher has been persuaded to republish that book so that it will be available probably the end of May, only in German. I mean, it should be in English, of course. But also, it should also be in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went through all the court records of when these poor people were tried, you know, these-- these trials in Nazi Germany. And there are terrible sentences. And what their lawyers did or didn't do-- many lawyers were very brave and tried to defend them. You know? And many of their crimes were not much worse than saying, well, certainly you realize-- to their congregation-- we're not going to win that war, you know, as the place was all bombed out all around them. You know? Then they'd be executed for that, or sent to a concentration camp or something. So she went through all that research. And now it's going to be republished. The Pope is coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one of our arguments. He's coming here the end of June, to visit Germany, and to make into some sort of a sanctifying-- how they make a saint, and proclaim as a soon-to-be saint or something. One of the priests who is in her books, a Berlin clergyman, [INAUDIBLE], who was really fantastically brave, the things he spoke up about to the congregation and they were reported. The Gestapo sits there and makes notes of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's in-- next day, he's in court. The next day, he's in a concentration camp. You know? He died on the way to the concentration camp. That was always a phrase that meant they executed him, you know. When you look back to the Nuremberg Trials, what, to your mind, was the biggest success? Or what, to your mind, was the biggest failure of the trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I lived in the shadow of this very good mind and specialized mind. You know? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, I have the same opinion. I think he felt that Nuremberg did a rather poor, pitiable job in public relations, in showing the Germans-- especially, he was thinking of the coming-- coming German republic-- in showing the Germans sufficiently that these-- these trials were-- you know what I mean? That the Germans had done-- their leaders had done these things. And-- inviting them more often. They were invited into the trial. But you know, making a bigger effort to make a wider knowledge acquaintance of Nuremberg among the German population. You know? But of course, we were not so well equipped. We didn't have so many people who had lived in Germany there. You know? And well, what would be the failure? I don't know. I think he felt that the great success was the exposing of the facts, which could have been suppressed or quietly pushed off in archives that would soon be burned quickly. You know? But you can't burn something after it's been on all these records. You know? It's still there, the records. And all the people, the very distinguished people, the very capable people that participated in that enormous project-- it's a testimony to the actuality of the whole thing. It's no swindle. It's-- it's the facts. And Nuremberg gradually is coming to have quite a reputation as a very fair trial. Because they could speak up and defend themselves. But here was their own documentation. You know? They had--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to get clarification on a point. You had mentioned that there have been changes in Germany over the years that you have seen. And you also made reference to 'Schindler's List,' the movie that Spielberg made. Have the changes come faster since that film has come out? Or is that irrelevant?

speaker: interviewee
text: That film had a big-- made a big impression. I would say it is a cascade of development since they-- but the thing was gradually developing. Many of these things, they turned their backs on 10 years after the trial. 20 years after the trial, it was a little bit more popular, step by step. And then, a very high point was President Richard von Weizsacker, who was a trial lawyer in defense of his helper of the law, team in charge of his father's defense. He made-- pulled the country forward in a great and much disputed-- a very brave and fine-- his-- his speech in 1985, when he called upon the country to be careful and remember carefully the Holocaust, and think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? I mean, it was a most unusual leadership and took a lot of civil courage to speak up and to make a sermon to the country. And it was greatly acclaimed and by large segments of the German population, recognized for what it was. The Germans have many very wonderful people. But under Hitler, they seemed to be just so suppressed. And they're a-- a very conservative group that don't-- they look what the-- what's the army doing? They don't get out of step with things, you know? And it's-- And another thing that we Americans-- I, as an American, where-- went to college with the help of the nuns in Rochester. It was becoming the-- the custom for women too, to also have a college education. And you were just beginning when you got through college, you know? Prior to that, high school had been the big game. Everybody had to get through high school. But in Germany, I noticed in Nazi Germany, when I was here, the famous German education, what is it? I don't know. I never took a statistic on it. Maybe 2% of the people get an education, the elite.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the great mass of Germans were given very few school years, and good little training as an apprentice in some trade. Which is admirable. But it doesn't make for political action, or political understanding, or the self-confidence to speak up when you feel that something is right or wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they often had-- some people say-- one argument went-- the reason-- everybody puzzles, how did this all happen? How could it happen? Civilized Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people's-- one line of argument is they had too good leadership in the past. Their kaisers were rather fine people, in a way. They want-- meant well for their country, and they would always think, well, I'm only a little man. You don't find Americans thinking so often, I'm only-- it was news to me to hear that people say, well, that's right. But-- And then I went to a conference with Dr. Kempner in 1947, in Heidelberg. And there, all of these learned Germans that still-- they were elderly. They had survived the war. And they were, some of them, back in teaching in Heidelberg, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they'd all talk, how could it happen? But the masses, they kept using the word the masses. Because the elite thought of all these people as people to whom they couldn't communicate. That was the masses. And we are the intellectuals and the political, knowledgeable people in-- in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now, with the help of Coca-Cola, the American-- exporting of the American educational system, which also benefited greatly from the refugees from German-- from Hitler Germany, our colleges and universities. Think only of the Aufbau and the New School for Social Research, and the different professors in our American universities in Berkeley and so forth. Good-- they brought it from Germany. And after the war, back came some-- many of these professors temporarily for brief courses, or even to resettle in Germany. They brought it back. But I often think the Chevrolet did a lot too.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German-- the-- the people didn't think of themselves as just, I'm just a little man, when they had a diploma. You know? Gradually, from the university too. More, and more, and more Germans go to the university and go on forever afterwards, become terrible scholars with footnotes, endless footnotes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, that plays a role in the political development of the country, the everybody feels they can speak up now or demonstrate. They love to demonstrate. And that's probably quite wholesome. Although once they demonstrated back in 1975, against Dr. Kempner. And that caused him to have a very bad heart attack. And he was an invalid for the rest of his life. They wanted Hess taken out and Hitler put in-- now, wait a minute. [LAUGHS] Hess taken out and Dr. Robert Kempner put in. And they demonstrated against Robert Kempner, and also against [INAUDIBLE] [? Cole, ?] who at that time was a minister president in one of the [INAUDIBLE] in Germany, in Mainz. And he refused to release from prison some war criminals that had big charges against them and big prison-- prison sentences. He refused to give them clemency and let them out of prison. And they were mad at him. And they were made at Robert Kempner, because he put Hess in prison. But of course, he didn't have any direct bearing on Hess being in prison. But as he always said, Hess was no innocent. He was in the Nuremberg racial laws, very much involved, in 1935.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Nurembergers had only the authority to try war criminals. That was a crime among the Germans. Because the Jews were German that were called Jews. You see? They were-- they-- their-- they lost their German rights. Which they had as much right to as Hess did, you know? But these racial laws took their whole citizenship away from them. They kind of more or less said, well, I guess we are Jews. You know? Which is not a political idea at all. In America it's a religion, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, these professors, back after the war, they puzzled over how things could have been done differently. And they said but the masses, what can you do? The masses don't understand. So I do think the educational system after the war has-- has been a big pillar in building up German democracy. I think it's very well entrenched now. There are radical [INAUDIBLE]. Some of them are anti-American or anti-this or anti-that. But I don't think they take root. Before we conclude our interview, is there anything you'd like to add? Anything which you'd forgotten to mention that you'd like to add now? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this role that Dr. Kempner played was called-- the-- the name they used was [GERMAN]. It means an adjunct prosecutor. It didn't have a status of a government employee, as a prosecutor is. And it didn't have a status of a private lawyer for a Mr. X or Y, who needs a lawyer to defend him, or prosecute, or litigate against someone. It was called an adjunct, a [GERMAN], [GERMAN] being a prosecutor. And these trials against war criminals played a big role in the years after the trials ended, in 1949. And trial after trial, they would gather the evidence, over 10, 15 years, together. And then, Dr. Kempner would come as a [GERMAN]. He had a client who was related to one of the victims, or so who is authorized to ask for a word to be spoken up for his client, you know. And as soon as they heard Dr. Kempner was coming, right away, the doctor would be brought out. The man was threatened with a heart attack. The trial would end the next day, suspended, the man not capable of standing trial. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? His name would-- they-- he had a kind of-- I don't know what you could call it. I don't know, maybe you would know of a word.

speaker: interviewee
text: They thought he knew everything. He didn't. He had great good fortune.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew the thing when it was necessary. It was just an accident that that happened-- he happened-- because he was so intensely involved with these things. He would think of that argument. And they kind of got the feeling he's carrying archives around on his person. You know what I mean? And it just happened. He had that atmosphere around him for the clients. They dreaded to see him come into the courtroom, you know? It was-- and they got a little weaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: They thought, we'll lose this. We better try to plead sick, which seems to be a wonderful plea, maybe all over the world. I don't know. If you're too sick to stand trial, you know, maybe the trial ends. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said he came to be known as Mr. Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in an editorial a couple of times, some of the German papers, Mr. Nuremberg came. They had a Nuremberg anniversary, the 40th anniversary, in the city of Nuremberg. And they came back from France, from Russia. Dr. Kempner came back, and big group of people. Endless scholars who had done their doctorates in all these countries on this thing. And most of the Nurembergers are not there anymore. And he got up and he started his speech. He said, I feel very low lonesome. I see, in this big audience, only one man that I ever saw in Nuremberg. And that was a professor Fried who-- I don't think he's alive now. I'm not sure, but he's a very old man, and he was there. And I feel very lonesome in this trial. There are only scholars that have been studying here. And we were in Nuremberg then. Nuremberg had changed, as you can imagine. The walls were on Nuremberg again. It wasn't all the exposed bathtubs and bombed out houses, and so forth. But the city was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for speaking with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you let me tell about my hobby, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. We'll have you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. I did. I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this book is not in English as yet. But I hope it soon will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very good--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --biography.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll show it one minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: This book is unusual. It's the memoirs of Robert Kempner, published in 1983. It was done in an unusual way. It was a series of interviews by a young reporter of Dr. Kempner. And he had these interviews taped by the publishing office. And the tape was typed-- typed into a type script and was never changed. It's just, so to speak, the raw series of lectures of Robert Kempner on his life, which spanned almost the century. He was born a few weeks before the new century, 1900, began. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a copy of a marvelous book that a great German statesman had prepared to congratulate Robert Kempner on his 90th birthday. He wrote-- this former prime minister of Rheinland Pfalz-- wrote to various political figures in Germany that Dr. Kempner had had experience with, and asked them to write, for this book, letters for his 90th birthday. And they are beautiful letters-- also some from America and France-- telling, almost always, something about how Dr. Kempner had made great contributions to the development of postwar Germany and postwar Europe through his continuous educational and exemplary work regarding anti-Nazism, which was his lifelong work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A few weeks before he died, a wonderful German book was published as a kind of spiritual or political restitution to the lawyers, the Jewish lawyers, or the lawyers of Jewish derivation, that had been abused by Nazi Germany. The-- these lawyers had played a great role in German law history, but had been taken off the law shelves and-- and thrown out of their work by Nazi Germany. And they covered the first Supreme Court Justice of Germany, back in the 1870s. And the last one in the book is Robert Kempner. There were about 50 lawyers who were reviewed in this book. And it was a work of many years' effort done by present-day German lawyers and presented to Dr. Kempner, shortly before he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what newspaper this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the 'Frankfurter Allgemeine,' 'Frankefurter Allgemeine Zeitung.' It is-- well, can I say it is something like 'The New York Times' of America? It is the standard German newspaper published and sent out internationally, and quite a nice attitude toward, I have always felt, toward America, and toward the development of-- of the new German republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is from December of 1993. Could you translate the headline for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: German law and the emancipation of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Eternal Vigilance is the Price of Liberty,' is the name-- often quoted by Dr. Kempner-- is the name given a big exhibition that travels these days around German's-- through German cities, staying, each time, several weeks in a location. And tells the story of his life-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his life-- telling the story of his life in postwar Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This lovely picture is Robert Kempner with his grandson, Robert II, second, when he was four or five years old, enjoying his little pipe. And he-- it's-- I think it shows that he really enjoyed his private life very much. And actually, he was a survivor, Robert Kempner. He had to survive many difficulties, and he did it with a lot of spirit and a lot of fight. And was glad to get to be 90 years old. He died at the age of 93. Felt that was a-- winning out over the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, oh, I would say close to 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sweden. The little boy grew up to be a Swedish farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
